pain_label: Years of trying everything — nothing sticks
trigger: Reaching a breaking point after years of bad sleep with no lasting solution from any remedy
desire: Something that actually works permanently, not another temporary fix
representative_quote: The program in this app ended a decade long stretch of insomnia. Before finding this I tried everything from supplements (magnesium, melatonin) to prescription medication to lifestyle changes (no caffeine, exercise more, etc) and nothing really worked.
distinct_from_existing: Distinct from Pill Escape Seeker in that the primary frustration is the futility of trial-and-error, not medication dependence. Tried melatonin AND meditation AND lifestyle changes AND pills — exhausted all paths.

pain_label: Literal weeks or months of zero sleep
trigger: Sleep tracking device confirming 0 hours slept for days/weeks — the physical collapse of not sleeping
desire: Any sleep at all — willing to try anything after hitting physical breaking point
representative_quote: I found Slumber One at a point in my life where I hadn't slept for weeks. I mean that literally. I track my sleep and I had gone for at least 3 weeks where Fitbit told me I literally had slept 0 minutes and 0 hours.
distinct_from_existing: More acute/crisis version of the Everything-Failer. The trigger is a physical limit being reached — not just years of bad sleep but recent total sleep loss. Often involves sleep-tracking data as evidence.

pain_label: Dreading bedtime — the fear cycle makes it worse
trigger: Realizing the anxiety about not sleeping is itself the main cause of not sleeping
desire: To not dread bedtime anymore — to feel neutral or comfortable about going to bed
representative_quote: I suffered from insomnia both when my son was born and again in perimenopause. It became so bad I would panic at bedtime, knowing I wouldn't get adequate rest. I knew it had become mental at this point as well and I needed help.
distinct_from_existing: Pure sleep-anxiety presentation — the insomnia is 100% the anxiety, not a physical or lifestyle issue. Key signal is 'panic at bedtime' language. Sleeping pills never worked because the issue is psychological.

pain_label: Mind won't turn off — thoughts keep coming
trigger: Night after night of lying awake with racing thoughts, even when exhausted
desire: Mental quiet at night — a brain that cooperates when the body wants to sleep
representative_quote: I like the emphasis on building skills that I can use to tackle my specific sleep issues vs other apps I've tried which try to just calm you down with generic seeming meditation. I've found that a lot of my sleep problems are made worse by my anxiety, and Stellar sleep has helped me recognize that and handle it in a way where it no longer affects my sleep.
distinct_from_existing: These users know meditation doesn't work for them — their anxiety is too active for passive relaxation. They need cognitive tools, not soundscapes. Key frustration with competitors: generic meditation apps don't address the cognitive component.

pain_label: Fear of dependency — need sleep without pills
trigger: Doctor prescribing sleep medication or noticing tolerance building — realizing they don't want to become dependent
desire: Sleep independence — to not need any external substance to sleep
representative_quote: I finally went to my new doctor, I had switched doctors because the last one really didn't address why I couldn't sleep and prescribed meds that didn't work, the new dr diagnosed me with anxiety due to being a caregiver for my husband fighting stage 4 cancer and was prescribed medication for anxiety and Temazepam for sleep, which usually worked, but I didn't want to get addicted.
distinct_from_existing: Often co-presents with The Chronic Everything-Failer (pills were one of the things they tried). Distinct trigger: fear of addiction/dependency, not just pill efficacy failure. Healthcare context is common — doctor referral or prescription is the entry point.

pain_label: Convinced sleep is a physical problem — skeptical that 'talking about it' helps
trigger: Having tried all physical solutions (melatonin, sleep hygiene, sleep trackers) with no success, finally trying psychology out of desperation
desire: To be proven wrong — to find out why they can't sleep when the 'obvious' solutions don't work
representative_quote: Initially, I approached Stellar Sleep with a healthy dose of skepticism. However, the scientifically supported methods the app employed to tackle sleep issues were compelling. The program felt different; it was more akin to a personal sleep coach, educating me on psych techniques to help me discover the deep-seated reasons for my sleep issues.
distinct_from_existing: This persona is often an overlap with Chronic Everything-Failer — the 'skeptic' is usually someone who tried everything physical and is now begrudgingly trying the psychological. The distinct sub-pain is the cultural resistance to 'therapy' framing for a sleep problem.

pain_label: Other sleep apps just played sounds — not addressing the real problem
trigger: Failure of Calm, Headspace, white noise, sleep stories — and the realization that relaxation ≠ fixing insomnia
desire: An app that actually teaches skills, not just 'calms down' the user
representative_quote: I like the emphasis on building skills that I can use to tackle my specific sleep issues vs other apps I've tried which try to just calm you down with generic seeming meditation.
distinct_from_existing: Competitor-switching signal: these users explicitly name other apps that failed (Calm, Headspace, generic meditation). The wedge vs. competitor apps is that Stellar Sleep teaches, not just soothes.

pain_label: Charged without warning after trial ends
trigger: Seeing a $130-$188 charge on credit card after believing they had cancelled or that the trial would not auto-renew
desire: Refund and a transparent, easy-to-cancel subscription model
representative_quote: This app would be great BUT READ THE REVIEWS about fraudulent billing. I did the 7-day free trial. Cancelled on day 7. Come to find out that 3 months later, my account was *somehow* still active when my credit card was charged $188.
distinct_from_existing: Not a buying persona — a churn/refund signal. But important: many of these users acknowledge the app content is good. The billing failure is what drives the 1★. Key insight: if billing friction were resolved, many of these would be 4-5★ reviews.

pain_label: Price hidden behind onboarding — sticker shock after investment
trigger: Completing the sleep quiz and onboarding flow before discovering the $188 price point
desire: Transparent pricing upfront so they can decide before investing time and personal data
representative_quote: They don't tell you before you download that the trial is as low as $0.99 but then it's $188 for 3 months. I desperately need help sleeping but I can't afford that.
distinct_from_existing: The funnel design forces emotional investment (quiz, vulnerability about sleep problems) before revealing the price. The betrayal feeling is amplified by the investment already made.
